title: Sampling from the Prior Distribution
concept: Because the latent space was trained to match a standard Gaussian, we can simply draw random numbers from that same Gaussian to get valid latent codes.

exercises.prompt: If a machine learns that most of its 'idea codes' cluster in a neat bell-shaped pattern, what's the easiest way to invent a brand-new idea?

options.text: Pick a random point from that same bell-shaped pattern
Copy an old idea code exactly
Only use the very edge of the pattern

exercises.explain: Since the model organizes its ideas in a bell-curve (Gaussian) shape, sampling randomly from that same shape gives valid new ideas!

cards.title: Rolling the Dice on Imagination
cards.body: What if creating a brand-new face, painting, or song was as easy as rolling a special kind of dice? Generative models make this possible by letting us 'roll' random numbers from a shape they already know well: the bell curve!

cards.title: Sampling from the Prior Distribution
cards.body: During training, a generative model squishes all its latent codes into a standard Gaussian (bell-curve) shape. Because of this, we can generate new latent codes just by drawing random numbers from that same standard Gaussian — no need to copy real data!

cards.title: The Dartboard of Ideas
cards.body: Imagine a dartboard shaped like a bell curve, where most darts land near the center and fewer land far away. If you know darts naturally fall in this pattern, you can predict where a new dart will land just by using the same pattern — that's exactly how we 'sample' new latent codes!

cards.title: Generating a New Face
cards.body: A face-generating AI learns to squeeze all face 'codes' into a standard Gaussian shape. To make a brand-new face, we just grab a random point from that same Gaussian and feed it to the decoder — out pops a face that's never existed before!

cards.title: Myth: You Need Real Data to Generate New Data
cards.body: Nope! Once training is done, we don't need any real examples to create something new. We just need random numbers from the standard Gaussian shape — the model handles turning that randomness into something meaningful.

cards.title: Training vs. Generating
cards.body: During training, the model learns to organize codes into a bell-curve shape. During generating, we just sample fresh points from that same shape — no training data needed!

left.text: Model squishes real data's latent codes into a standard Gaussian shape

right.text: We draw fresh random numbers from that same Gaussian to create new data

exercises.prompt: Why can we sample random numbers from a standard Gaussian to generate new latent codes?

options.text: Because the model was trained so its latent space matches that same Gaussian shape
Because Gaussians are the only shape computers understand
Because it makes the training faster
Because real data is always shaped like a Gaussian

exercises.explain: The latent space is specifically trained to match a standard Gaussian, so drawing random points from it gives us valid, usable latent codes.

exercises.prompt: You need access to the original training images to generate new samples from a trained generative model.
exercises.explain: False! Once trained, you only need random numbers from the standard Gaussian — no original data required.

exercises.sentence: Because the latent space matches a standard ___, we can sample random numbers from it to create new data.
exercises.explain: The model's latent space is shaped like a standard Gaussian, so sampling from that same distribution gives valid latent codes.

exercises.prompt: A student says: 'To generate a new image, I need to average a bunch of real training images together.' What's wrong with this idea?

options.text: Nothing — averaging real images is exactly how generative models create new samples
Generative models instead sample random points from the learned Gaussian latent space and decode them — no averaging of real images needed
You should subtract images instead of averaging them
Real images can't be used at all, ever

exercises.explain: The correct method is sampling random latent codes from the standard Gaussian shape, then decoding them — not averaging real images.

exercises.prompt: A generative model was trained so its latent codes form a standard Gaussian shape. You want to create a completely new, never-before-seen output. What should you do?

options.text: Pick a random point from the standard Gaussian and feed it into the decoder
Find the exact latent code of a training example and reuse it
Retrain the entire model from scratch every time
Only use the latent code that's exactly at the center of the Gaussian

exercises.explain: Since the latent space matches a standard Gaussian, we generate new content by sampling a random point from that Gaussian and decoding it — this gives us fresh, valid, never-before-seen outputs every time.
